Business Requirements document.ChecklistItem: Document has a clear title, version, author, and date.
Includes a document control and revision history section.
Document purpose and audience are clearly stated.
References related documents (e.g., project charter,  specs).
Business drivers and context are explained.
Benefits and expected outcomes are described.
Stakeholders, roles, and responsibilities are identified.
Stakeholder needs and expectations are captured.
In-scope processes, systems, and functions are clearly listed.
Out-of-scope items are explicitly stated.
Scope aligns with project charter or business case.
Each requirement is uniquely identified and clearly written.
Requirements are SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
Requirements are categorized (e.g., functional, non-functional, reporting).
Business rules and logic are clearly documented.
As-Is and To-Be states are clearly described
Requirements are written in business-friendly language.
All relevant assumptions are listed and validated.
Constraints (e.g., time, resources, system limitations) are documented.
Dependencies on systems, processes, or teams are identified.
Interfaces and integration points are described.
Compliance with regulatory or industry standards is considered.
Clear acceptance criteria are provided for each requirement.
Requirements are mapped to source documents or business goals.
List of Open and Closed points to be documented properly.
Reviewed by all relevant stakeholders.
Final version is approved and signed off.

Configuration Document.ChecklistItem: Document includes title, version, author, and date.
Revision history is maintained and accurate.
Purpose and scope of the document are clearly defined.
References related documents (e.g., BRD, FDS, test cases).
System (ERP) and specific module(s) are clearly identified.
Configuration environment (DEV, QA, PROD) is specified.
Configuration steps are described in detail.
Screen paths (menu navigation) and transaction codes are provided.
Field-level settings and values are clearly defined.
Custom settings vs. standard settings are distinguished.
Screenshots are included for clarity and verification.
Configuration supports defined business processes.
Any changes to standard process flow are explained.
Configuration decisions are justified with business rationale.
Master and transactional data dependencies are described.
Default values and mandatory fields are specified.
User roles impacted by configuration are listed.
Access controls for configuration settings are described.
Dependencies on other modules or systems are identified.
Potential downstream impacts are assessed.
Compliance with organizational and vendor standards is confirmed.
Document is peer-reviewed for accuracy and completeness.
Comments from reviewers are addressed.
Final version is approved and signed off.

Functional Design Document.ChecklistItem: Document has a clear title, version number
Correct Functional Design template is used
Includes a revision history with changes, Authors, approvers and dates
States the objective and scope of the custom development.
Lists related documents (e.g., BRD, Technical Specs, Process Flows).
All business requirements are clearly stated or referenced.
Functional design aligns with the approved business requirements.
Assumptions and constraints are clearly stated.
Process flows or diagrams are included and accurate.
Clearly defines input, process logic, and output.
All custom logic is documented with decision criteria of the business logic.
Lists down all the names of custom objects created like Process names, lookup, profile options etc
Lists down , describes exceptions and error handling scenarios
Process steps are traceable to business requirements

Functional Design Document.Section:
Form and User Interface Design 
(If applicable)
Functional Design Document.ChecklistItem: UI/UX elements are described and prototyped with mock up screen layouts

Functional Design Document.Section:
Form and User Interface Design 
(If applicable)
Functional Design Document.ChecklistItem: Field-level design, validations, and default values are defined

Functional Design Document.Section:
Form and User Interface Design 
(If applicable)
Functional Design Document.ChecklistItem: Navigation logic is clear and user-friendly
Report layout and content requirements are documented.
Filters, sorting, and grouping logic are defined.
The report field descriptions and derivation logic clearly documented
Output formats (PDF, Excel, etc.) are specified.
Master and transactional data requirements are identified and clearly documented
Source and target systems are documented along with the middleware details as applicable
Data mappings and transformation logic are included.
Integration points (APIs, middleware, interfaces) are specified
Required user roles and access levels are defined.
Sensitive data handling and visibility are addressed.
Complies with organizational security policies.
Includes functional acceptance criteria.
Lists test scenarios or test conditions for UAT.
Describes expected results for major use cases.
Identifies impact on existing processes or modules.
Lists dependencies on other customizations or systems.
Flags any obsolete or replaced functionality.
Follows ERP vendor’s development guidelines (e.g., SAP, Oracle).
Naming conventions, code structures, and documentation are standardized.
Adheres to internal governance and change management policies.
Document is peer-reviewed for accuracy and completeness.
Comments from reviewers are addressed.
Final version is approved and signed off.

Test Scripts.ChecklistItem: Test script includes unique ID, title, version, and author.
Clearly states the module, process area, and functionality under test.
Document includes preparer, reviewer, and approver sections.
Test script is traceable to related business requirement or user story.
Test objective and purpose are clearly defined.
Preconditions (prerequisites, setup conditions) are documented.
Test data required for execution is specified.
Step-by-step instructions are clear, numbered, and logical.
Expected results are defined for each step.
Pass/fail criteria are objective and measurable.
Includes postconditions (e.g., data cleanup, next steps).
Covers all relevant business scenarios (positive and negative).
Includes test cases for exceptions and boundary conditions.
All functional requirements are mapped to at least one test case.
Validates integrations and data flow where applicable.
Includes relevant workflow or approval processes.
Uses business terminology that end users understand.
Avoids ambiguous or overly technical language.
Includes relevant screenshots, forms, or references. Screenshot is mandatory for every test result
Formatting is consistent and readable (e.g., tables, bolding, bullets).
Test data is realistic and representative of production scenarios.
Environment (DEV, QA, UAT) required for testing is specified.
Dependencies on other systems or modules are clearly stated.
User roles and permissions for test execution are defined.
Instructions for logging defects are included.
Each test step provides traceability to test results or defect IDs.
Retesting or regression steps are documented if applicable.
Reviewed by both functional and technical stakeholders.
Peer-reviewed for completeness and accuracy.
Final script is approved and signed off for use in UAT or SIT.

Technical Design Document.ChecklistItem: Ensure the Technical Design Document (TRS/TDD) uses the standard project-approved template and includes version control.
Confirm that the document clearly defines the objective of the custom object (e.g., report, interface, extension).
The object should be tagged correctly (e.g., R-Report, I-Interface, etc.)
Diagrams (if applicable) showing data flow, integrations (OIC, REST, SOAP) , and dependencies.
The document should contain pseudocode or SQL logic, validations, joins, and conditions.
List of tables used, field-level mappings, and any DDL (for custom tables/views).
For reports/concurrent programs, confirm parameters and output formats are defined.
Explanation of how exceptions and failures will be managed (logs, alerts, retries).
Ensure user roles, data security, and masking (e.g., PII) are considered. VBCS Security model for extensions aligned with Fusion roles
Index usage, expected volumes, tuning tips, and long-running query identification.
File names, script execution order, and any required configuration setup.
Confirm that the design has been peer-reviewed and signed off by the Tech Lead.
Peer reviewer should be selected based on module/domain knowledge (e.g., SCM, FIN, HCM).
Peer review must happen before formal QA review is initiated.

SOW Document.ChecklistItem: Incase the currency is in INR, the Signing Entity should be RITE Softwrare Pvt Ltd.
1st paragraph of SOW must refer to the MSA document (with the date when MSA is exceuted).

SOW Document.Section: SOW Effective Dates & Disclaimer on MSA clauses
SOW Document.ChecklistItem: 2nd Paragraph to refer SOW with Effective date and standard clause

This section explains about
1. "What client is expecting from RITE?"
2. The business Requirement/ Problem the SOW is going to address

Clearly mention the Line level scope details.
If SOW covers Functiona/ Technical tasks, keep the details separate sub-sections.
Mention the Activities/ Roles by each Track
Incase the details are huge (like # of Reports, Data Migration components, Test script details etc.) -- Mention all of them in APPENDIX (at the End of SOW) and Reference to that from SCOPE section.

Incase of T&M, mention the Technologies/ Modules the Resource is going to work on

Mention the list of deliverables that we produce as part of the assignment.
Do not over commit.

Incase of T&M, mention the Technologies/ Modules the Resource is going to work on.

Clearly list down the General, Technical Assumptions. The list should include (not limited to) -- Involvement of client team (for requirements clarifications/ timely signoffs/ coordination with other partners etc.), Access/Privileges/ Connectivity details etc.

Mention the Project approach (whether Waterfall model/ Sprint)
Detail out the Milestones by Dates and Acceptance criterion.
Prefer to have Entry & Exit criterion for each Stage
List down the Role (NOT NAMES), Location (ONSITE/OFFSHORE/NEARSHORE), Role-Responsibilities.
Should have Start Date & End Date
Incase no content, mention Standard Clause " Any Activities or Deliverables NOT LISTED in SCOPE/ Deliverable Section"
List down the details that Client need to keep it Ready before we start the assignment. These kind of overlap with Assumptions.

1. If its Fixed Price --List the Milestone Description, Date, Amount. DO NOT SHARE RATES BY EACH RESOURCE, # of Resources & # of Months.
While listing the Milestones-- keep CASHFLOW in mind. i.e. Should have Milestones at Regular intervals (monthly or bi-monthly). Should not be only 1 milestone at the end of assignment
2. T&M -- Monthly Billing Amount (Mention by Each Role Wise, Hour Rate, # of Hours/Month, Monthly Bill Amount).

1. Tabular format for Contact Details of Management from both sides (RITE and CLIENT).
2. Should have two Rows at minimum--- Project Managers, 1st Level of Escalation.
3. Recommended to include Communication Schedule as well (Weekly Connect/ Daily Connect etc.)
Depends on SOW Signing Entity, Signatury Names to be present.

Test Plan.ChecklistItem: Project name, scope, Oracle Fusion modules list, implementation type.
SIT, UAT, Regression, OIC Testing, RICEW validation, Quarterly Patch Testing.
HCM, Finance, SCM, PPM, Procurement, Payroll, OTM, as applicable.
Validate configurations, integrations, workflows, security roles, reports, conversions.
Test Plan, Test Scenarios, Test Cases, RTM, Daily Status Reports, Test Summary Report.
SIT, UAT, PRE-PROD, PROD, including P2T/P3T refresh details.
Approved BRD/MD50, configurations completed, test data ready, environment provisioned.
All critical tests passed, major defects closed or accepted, UAT sign-off received.
Manual and Automation testing, OIC validations, RICEW testing, workflow validation, E2E testing.
QA Manager, Test Lead, Module Testers, Integration Testers, Automation Engineers, SMEs, Oracle Consultants.
Environment delays, data issues, dependency on integrations, lack of SME availability.
SIT/UAT cycles, execution windows, major milestones, test cycle duration.

Oracle Integration Code Review.Unnamed: 1: Checklist Item
Does the integration name align with naming standards?
Does the integration name address the business purpose?
Does The integration Versioning Defined Properly?
Does integration description added in Integration level?
Is the selected Integration pattern suitable and justified for the business solution?
Does integration have any hardcoded URL’s or credentials?
Is Proper tracking defined for the integrations?
Is integration placed in the correct folder structure or project?
Are lookups used instead of hardcoding?
Integration reusable?
Is Orchestration justified, or could it be simplified?
Does the connection name follows naming standards?
Did the connection used appropriate authentication method? (Like OAuth/Basic/API Key)
Did connections have hardcoded credentials?
Does the security policies are added for each connection?
For third party connection SSL Certification validated for expiry?
Are lookup names conforming to naming standards?
Is the lookup placed in the appropriate folder?
Are hard-coded values avoided and replaced with lookups where applicable?
Are lookup values properly documented in the design document?
Is there proper error handling for missing lookup or cross-reference values?
Are lookups environment-independent (no environment-specific hardcoding)?
Has impact analysis been performed before modifying existing lookup values?
Did the integration modularize the business logic appropriately?
Are scope blocks used appropriately within the integration?
Are fault handlers implemented where required?
Is a global fault handler configured for the integration?
Are switch conditions optimized for better performance and readability?
Are for-each loops optimized to handle data efficiently?
Does the integration avoid unnecessary nested loops?
Are data mappings clean, structured, and easy to understand?
Are lookup tables used instead of hard-coded values inside mapping?
Are cross-references used properly wherever required?
Has large payload handling been considered in the integration design?
Is retry logic implemented where external system failures may occur?
Does the global fault handler is implemented?
Does the error Logged for tracking ?
Does the retry mechanism implemented where required?
Are business errors and technical errors handled separately?
Are meaningful error messages returned for failure scenarios?
Does the integration avoid logging sensitive data?
Are notifications (Email/Teams) configured for failure scenarios?
Are integration instance tracking and business identifiers configured?
Does the integration avoid unnecessary database calls?
Does the integration avoid making external calls inside loops?
Is pagination used when processing large datasets?
Is parallel processing used where appropriate to improve performance?
Has high-volume testing been completed for the integration?
Is the payload size optimized for performance?
Is payload streaming or stage file used for very large files?
Are standard APIs used?
Are REST / SOAP / FBDI services used appropriately?
Is Business Unit / Multi-Org handling validated?
Is data validation performed before submission?
Is idempotency handled where required?
Are Unit test cases add for each integration?
Did negative scenarios are tested?
Did error scenarios are validated and tested to ensure proper error handling?
Has the deployment checklist been followed before migration?
Has the .IAR file been validated before migrating to the target environment?
Has a post-deployment smoke test been completed successfully?
Does the scheduling frequency configured appropriately based on business requirement?
Does the time zone been validated for the scheduling integration?
Does the alerts are configured for integration failures?
Did the overlapping integration run prevention implemented?
Are naming standards followed across all integration artifacts?
Are there any test endpoints present in the Production environment?
Does the integration avoid environment-specific hardcoding?
Is the documentation updated to reflect the latest changes?
Is the change ticket referenced in the integration?
Has the integration version been updated before migration?
Do all names conform to standards?
Does functional Documentation exist? Is it clear and complete?
Does the TDD Document cover all the required details along with flow chart?
Does testcase document cover all the positive and negative scenarios and been tested?

OIC Naming Conventions & Best Practices.Unnamed: 1: Checklist Item
All names are spelled out fully without unapproved abbreviations (exceptions include common accepted terms like ERP, HCM, AR, AP, SF).
Artifact names use only alphanumeric characters and underscores or Pascal Case.
No special characters are used in names (avoid spaces, '.', '$', '%', '#', []).
Artifact types are clearly indicated using suffixes (e.g., COUNTRIES_LOOKUP).
Project names conform to standard convention `<CUST_PREFIX>_<MODULE_NAME>_PROJECT`.
Common utility assets are grouped in a common project `<CUST_PREFIX>_COMMON_PROJECT`.
Integration Flow names conform strictly to `<CUST_PREFIX>_<INT_NUM>_<SOURCE>_<TARGET>_<DATA_OBJECT>_<OPERATION>_<TYPE>`.
Connection names adhere to `<CUST_PREFIX>_<SYSTEM>_<ADAPTER_NAME>` and duplication is minimal.
Lookups properly indicate their short purpose and end with `_LKP` prefix.
Initial lookup variables are assigned locally instead of mapped directly inside flow blocks.
Assignment tasks are prefixed with 'Assign' (e.g., AssignOrderValues).
Data Stitch tasks denote their operation via 'Assign', 'Append', or 'Remove'.
Scope blocks are explicitly labeled with suffix `Scope` or `{Scope}`.
For-each iteration blocks begin with `IterateOver` or `ForEach`.
While loops begin with `While` and specify the condition concisely.
Logger blocks begin with `Log` to clearly designate what is being logged.
Notification modules start with `Email` followed by topic context.
Lower-Camel-case is maintained for all XML attributes.
Upper-Camel-case is maintained for all XML complex elements and type names.
Names remain singular unless strictly representing arrays.
All complex schemas maintain the 'Type' suffix descriptor (e.g., InvoiceEBOType).
Integration relies on appropriate shared central connections rather than creating redundant developer connections.
Scheduled routines use list limits appropriately and don't loop endlessly; run-now triggers or schedule params are utilized where possible.
Synchronous integration complexity is minimized to basic handoffs; avoids performing large monolithic processing inside blocking calls.
Processors don't buffer massive entire payload files into memory; Stage File chunking / Segment iteration is preferred.
Chatty per-record API invocations are avoided; external API batched calls (200, 1000 records context) are mapped.
IAR updates or complex XSLs occur strictly within Mapper import functions, bypassing manual XML outside edits.
Underlying DB / REST limits are analyzed in line with payload estimates to avoid abrupt buffer limits.
